jokes.text: She'll think it's, like, a kooky necklace holder or something. She'll never think it's a dildo.
jokes.setup: Ilana is helping Abbi tidy up before her mom arrives; something is hanging on the wall that is clearly a dildo.
jokes.punchline: Ilana confidently asserts Abbi's conservative mom will mistake a dildo for a necklace holder — a transparently absurd claim that reveals Ilana's obliviousness to social norms.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly in character for Ilana — her breezy confidence that something obviously sexual will read as innocuous is a reliable comedic engine for her character. The phrasing 'kooky necklace holder' does real work. Not exceptional but very well-executed character comedy.

jokes.text: Oof, that hurts my heart.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just said Abbi's mom will never think the dildo is a dildo.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's wounded, resigned reaction — not to the embarrassment of having a dildo on the wall, but to the implication her mom doesn't know her well enough to recognize her vibe — lands as self-aware cringe.
jokes.explanation: Brief but effective. The economy is the joke's strength — four words that communicate character, self-awareness, and a comedic reframe of why the remark stings. Abbi's slightly wounded dignity is her best comedic register.

jokes.text: Just 'cause I don't call my mom 'Abbi's Mom.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has made a banner or sign labeled 'Abbi's Mom' as a welcome decoration; Abbi says she feels like her mom will know Abbi made it.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that the sign literally says 'Abbi's Mom' — the most literal, unimaginative label possible — is the joke. It's also a visual gag (audience sees the sign).
jokes.explanation: This earns its 8+ because it's a perfect encapsulation of Ilana's character through a single visual object. The joke is structurally elegant: Abbi raises an abstract concern (her mom will know she made it), and the reveal explains WHY in the most concrete, absurd way possible. 'Abbi's Mom' as a formal title is wonderfully childlike and also the episode's title card origin, giving it meta weight. The economy is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: And then top it all off with a fancy dinner at Sushi Mambeaux!
jokes.setup: Ilana is listing the itinerary she's planned for Abbi's mom's visit — frozen hot chocolate, Santa at Macy's — building an impression of a wholesome, touristy day.
jokes.punchline: The list escalates to a restaurant with a deliberately ridiculous fake-fancy name ('Sushi Mambeaux') that signals the episode's setting and Ilana's overreach.
jokes.explanation: The joke is mostly the name 'Sushi Mambeaux' — a portmanteau of upscale French and Japanese dining that sounds exactly like something a try-hard NYC restaurant would call itself. Functional worldbuilding comedy.

jokes.text: So, I appreciate you cutting the cheese.
jokes.setup: Ilana is praising Abbi's cheese board she made for her conservative mom, meaning Abbi literally cut cheese for a charcuterie display.
jokes.punchline: Ilana uses 'cutting the cheese' — the euphemism for flatulence — entirely sincerely, without realizing the double meaning, immediately followed by her catching herself.
jokes.explanation: A classic double-entendre setup that works because of the genuine context — Abbi really did cut cheese. The follow-through ('Cut the cheese, shit! I haven't said that since 1997') is where the laugh lives. Not especially original but well-executed.

jokes.text: Cut the cheese, shit! I haven't said that since, like, 1997.
jokes.setup: Ilana just said 'cutting the cheese' and catches herself.
jokes.punchline: Her embarrassed correction — not 'sorry, I didn't mean it that way' but pride-tinged embarrassment that she used an outdated slang term — reframes the awkwardness as generational.
jokes.explanation: The callback to joke 5 within seconds adds escalation. The specific year '1997' does work — it's concrete and absurd. Ilana's self-correction being about the term's datedness rather than its meaning is character-specific.

jokes.text: Oh! Whoa! Yes, oh, my God! Thank God, I'm so sorry about that. Whoo! Okay. Back, baby, I'm back!
jokes.setup: Ilana turns on a SAD lamp and the light hits her face.
jokes.punchline: Her reaction is orgasmic, almost religious — as if the light is a drug hit. The visual of her basking in a therapy lamp like it's a spotlight from God is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's physicality is a major comedy asset and her treating the SAD lamp like a hit of ecstasy is pure character. 'Back, baby, I'm back!' is the kind of line that works because of delivery more than writing. High impact, good character integration.

jokes.text: Thank science for SAD lamps. Why don't they just call them happy lamps 'cause, like, sad lamps sounds so... sad.
jokes.setup: Ilana is grateful for SAD lamps but immediately questions their naming.
jokes.punchline: The logic is circular and childlike — 'sad lamps sounds sad' — and the pause before 'sad' is a self-aware beat. 'Thank science' instead of 'thank God' is also a small character note.
jokes.explanation: 'Thank science' is the best joke in this beat — it's a small but precise character note for Ilana. The sad/happy lamp observation is familiar territory but the delivery saves it.

jokes.text: It stands for 'Seasonal Affective Disorder,' which indicates the annual cycle of depression that I and others like me experience every winter, all winter. And fall. And, like, uh... the end of summer too.
jokes.setup: Ilana defensively explains what SAD stands for, starting with clinical authority.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'all winter' to 'and fall' to 'the end of summer' gradually reveals she is depressed almost year-round, undercutting her earlier confident health claims.
jokes.explanation: This escalation is genuinely well-constructed. She starts clinical and authoritative, then the additions ('and fall... and the end of summer too') come as self-aware admissions that undermine her credibility in real time. The comedy is in watching someone try to maintain dignity while confessing an expanding problem. Strong structural work.

jokes.text: It's that sadness. You know, that chews us up and spits us out into the black abyss.
jokes.setup: Ilana is explaining her depression mid-lamp-session.
jokes.punchline: The sudden tonal shift to gothic, dramatic language ('black abyss') while basking in a therapy lamp creates tonal whiplash that's darkly funny.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Ilana's ecstatic physical state (on the lamp) and the dark content of her words is the engine here. 'Black abyss' is melodramatic enough to be funny without being cartoonish.

jokes.text: Ilana, maybe you just increase your medication? No, no, no, I don't need to do that. 'Cause I have been steadily decreasing my dosage of antidepressants for two years.
jokes.setup: Abbi cautiously suggests Ilana increase her antidepressant dosage given what she just witnessed.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's confident refusal, backed by the reveal that she has been doing the exact opposite — decreasing her dosage for two years — is the joke. She is emphatically wrong and doesn't know it.
jokes.explanation: Strong irony: the person who most clearly needs more medication is the one decreasing it. The joke also functions as genuine character depth — Ilana's relationship with mental health medication is real and complicated in the show. This earns its score through character integration and structure.

jokes.text: Remember that?! And it is working like a charm. Yeah, seems like it's working. Well...
jokes.setup: Ilana references the happy lamp with pride, asserting her self-treatment is working.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's dry 'Yeah, seems like it's working' — delivered with obvious skepticism given what just happened — is the joke. The hanging 'Well...' is a reaction beat that does the heavy lifting.
jokes.explanation: Abbi's straight-man dry skepticism is a reliable engine and it fires well here. Economy is the strength — she says very little but it communicates everything.

jokes.text: She'll be okay. [beat/pause]
jokes.setup: Abbi says to camera (or to herself) that Ilana will be fine, following everything we just witnessed.
jokes.punchline: The audience has just seen significant evidence to the contrary. The line is a textbook deadpan-to-camera reassurance that functions as denial.
jokes.explanation: Three words delivering maximum ironic distance. Works as a scene button. The pause after ('♪♪') amplifies the comedic doubt. Simple but effective.

jokes.text: My little garbage pail girl!
jokes.setup: Abbi's conservative mom arrives at the door. We expect a warm but conventional greeting.
jokes.punchline: She immediately deploys 'garbage pail girl' as a term of endearment, signaling she is more idiosyncratic than the 'conservative mom' framing suggested.
jokes.explanation: 'Garbage pail girl' is a specific, weird, loving insult that immediately establishes Joanne as a character with personality — and retroactively reframes Abbi's 'she's conservative' warning. The specificity of 'Garbage Pail Kids' as a reference is doing real work here. Strong character introduction.

jokes.text: Okay, it was just a phase. For a couple years.
jokes.setup: Abbi's mom calls her 'garbage pail girl.'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's defensive qualification — first denying ('just a phase'), then undermining her own denial ('for a couple years') — is the classic Abbi self-sabotage structure.
jokes.explanation: The two-beat structure — denial then qualification — is a well-worn pattern but Abbi executes it cleanly. 'A couple years' is specific enough to be funny.

jokes.text: Oh, gosh, everything looks so clean. Yeah. It's basically just some glue, toothpicks. And I just did little designs 'cause I wanted you to see what kinds of cheeses that it was.
jokes.setup: Joanne admiringly says the apartment looks clean; Abbi thinks she's complimenting the cheese board she made.
jokes.punchline: Abbi launches into an overly detailed explanation of her craft cheese board — 'glue, toothpicks' — as if justifying her artistic choices, while also subtly deflecting from the cleanliness comment (implying the apartment isn't usually clean).
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe beat. Abbi over-explaining her cheese board is characteristic — her need for approval from her mom activates her anxious oversharing mode. Not spectacular but does characterization work.

jokes.text: So, what are we gonna do today? Well, you know, I would love to take you to my studio space. Uh, but I'm actually renting it out, so...
jokes.setup: Joanne has just praised Abbi for 'living as an artist in your perfect apartment in the big city.' Abbi basks in this, then is asked about plans.
jokes.punchline: Abbi immediately reveals she has rented out her studio — the physical embodiment of her artist identity — to make ends meet, deflating the 'living as an artist' mythology in real time.
jokes.explanation: The joke is structural: mom praises the artist life, then Abbi instantly reveals the artist life is a fiction she's maintaining. 'Uh, but I'm actually renting it out, so...' delivered with the trailing 'so' is a signature Abbi verbal tic of embarrassed explanation. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Phie is the name of the artist that I'm renting it to, Sophie. She is just, like, she's super funky.
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to justify renting out her studio by making it sound cool.
jokes.punchline: She deploys 'super funky' — a word so vague and desperate it's its own joke — while immediately being undercut by her mom.
jokes.explanation: Abbi's over-justification instinct is running here. The joke is in the effort — 'super funky' is exactly the kind of desperate spin Abbi would put on a humiliating situation.

jokes.text: Abbi, we need to be honest with each other. Yeah, you know what, she's not, she's not that funky. She's, like, a pretty mainstream artist. Abbi, stop. I meant me. I need to be honest with you.
jokes.setup: Mom says 'we need to be honest with each other' — triggering Abbi's self-protective spiral about Sophie being 'mainstream.'
jokes.punchline: The misdirection is complete: Abbi preemptively starts confessing something inconsequential (Sophie's mainstream art), while her mom was building to something genuinely serious. The structure mirrors Abbi's anxiety — she deflects from potential real conversation with irrelevant chatter.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed misdirection. 'We need to be honest' is a universal cringe trigger, and Abbi's instinct to immediately fill the air with irrelevant self-justification is both very funny and deeply true to the character. The mom's patient 'Abbi, stop' lands perfectly. The structure is tight and the character integration is exceptional — this moment couldn't happen with anyone but Abbi.

jokes.text: I've never, um, had a martini. I never went to a play at night. Matinees. I've never had a pineapple barbecue pizza thing from Hawaii. I've never gone without underwear. I wanna wear clothes meant for black women.
jokes.setup: Joanne, having revealed the benign lump scare, lists things she wants to do before she dies.
jokes.punchline: The bucket list escalates from understandable (martini, night play) to odd (underwear-free) to completely unexpected ('I wanna wear clothes meant for black women') — the final item landing as a non-sequitur that reveals a deeply specific unfulfilled desire.
jokes.explanation: The list structure is a classic comedy escalation device, and this one executes it well. Each item is funnier than the last, with 'I wanna wear clothes meant for black women' being the spike — it's specific, weird, socially loaded in a way the show engages with directly, and perfectly character-specific to a suburban conservative white mom who has suppressed herself. Abbi's 'That's an interesting one' is the ideal button.

jokes.text: That's an interesting one.
jokes.setup: Joanne has just said she wants to 'wear clothes meant for black women' as part of her bucket list.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's perfectly calibrated non-reaction — not endorsing it, not challenging it, just noting it with diplomatic neutrality — is funnier than any direct response could be.
jokes.explanation: Four words that do enormous work. The restraint is the craft here — Abbi knows exactly how to not engage with her mom's problematic items while still landing a joke. Economy score is the highest possible because there is nothing to add or remove.

jokes.text: Did you call me?! Bevers! No! What? Oh, shh, uh... Bevers, Jesus!
jokes.setup: Joanne has just asked Abbi for a 'naughty girls' night out' — an intimate, vulnerable request. The scene is emotionally loaded.
jokes.punchline: Bevers bursts in, presumably underdressed, shattering the emotional moment. His first line ('Did you call me?!') implies he's been listening for his name — a perfectly pathetic character beat.
jokes.explanation: Bevers is a reliable comedic device and his intrusion into a vulnerable moment is well-timed. 'When do I ever call you?' from Abbi is the best line in this beat — it summarizes their entire dynamic in five words.

jokes.text: When do I ever call you?
jokes.setup: Bevers says he thought Abbi called him.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's flat rhetorical question is both a burn and a character revelation — she clearly never willingly interacts with Bevers.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy. Six words that define a character relationship completely.

jokes.text: Well, I'm Joanne. Joanne? I'm Matthew. Or Abbi's mom is how you should introduce yourself.
jokes.setup: Bevers introduces himself by first name to Abbi's mom.
jokes.punchline: Abbi immediately corrects Joanne's introduction to 'Abbi's mom' — a callback to the 'Abbi's Mom' banner joke, now repurposed as Abbi asserting control by reducing her mother to a function rather than a person.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the episode's opening joke lands beautifully here. Ilana made a sign calling her 'Abbi's Mom,' and now Abbi — in a panic — uses the same reductive framing to assert authority over the situation. It's structurally satisfying and adds a layer to the opening joke in retrospect.

jokes.text: Joanne, until next time.
jokes.setup: Abbi tells Bevers to put on clothes or stay in his room.
jokes.punchline: Bevers graciously says goodbye to Joanne as if he's a proper host parting with a guest — entirely oblivious to the awkwardness. The formality of 'until next time' from a man in no pants is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between the formality of 'until next time' and Bevers's actual situation (being kicked out of the room, presumably undressed) is pure Bevers. It's a character-specific absurdity.

jokes.text: Oh, well... Okay. Yes, until next time. Your scarf. Thanks. Cute. Mom? Furry little bear.
jokes.setup: Bevers has said 'until next time' to Joanne after being dismissed.
jokes.punchline: Joanne warmly reciprocates — 'Yes, until next time' — then hands him his scarf, and Abbi watches helplessly as her mom charms with her intruding roommate's boyfriend. 'Furry little bear' as Joanne's departing nickname for Bevers is the final note.
jokes.explanation: 'Furry little bear' from a conservative mom who just met a near-naked man is a delightful character note for Joanne. It signals she is going to be more fun than expected — and that Abbi has no control over this day.

jokes.text: Bonjour! Listen up, hunties. It's that time of year. Spring cleaning.
jokes.setup: Marcel addresses his restaurant staff in a manager's meeting.
jokes.punchline: 'Spring cleaning' as a restaurant manager's term turns out to mean firing staff, not literal cleaning — and 'hunties' as a term of address for employees sets the tone for Marcel as a character.
jokes.explanation: Marcel's introduction is effective. 'Hunties' is a culturally specific term of address that immediately characterizes him. The subversion of 'spring cleaning' into a competitive firing scheme is a solid misdirection.

jokes.text: I owe so much money to so many bad people. I'll need two weeks because I have to relocate.
jokes.setup: Marcel announces that the lowest tip-earner will be fired.
jokes.punchline: An unnamed waiter's aside reveals a catastrophically bad personal situation — owing money to dangerous people, needing to flee — framed as a reaction to the possibility of job loss, implying the job was the only thing keeping disaster at bay.
jokes.explanation: This background character gets one of the episode's better one-off jokes. The specificity — 'bad people' and 'relocate' — implies a whole noir backstory in ten words. Dark comedy at its most economical.

jokes.text: You are all enemies now.
jokes.setup: Marcel has announced the competition structure.
jokes.punchline: Declaring coworkers 'enemies' with flat finality, as if this is a logical HR conclusion, is perfectly deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum impact. Marcel delivering this with managerial authority is what makes it work. Economy score is perfect — there is nothing to add.

jokes.text: Have you guys ever fallen asleep here and just spent the night? If you ever have to, it's surprisingly not soul crushing.
jokes.setup: Ilana is at the restaurant, presumably prepping for the evening.
jokes.punchline: Her casual admission that she has slept at the restaurant, framed as a helpful practical tip ('surprisingly not soul crushing'), is simultaneously sad and funny — the low bar of 'not soul crushing' as a recommendation.
jokes.explanation: 'Surprisingly not soul crushing' is a very good phrase — it's the kind of endorsement that damns with faint praise while also revealing genuine rock-bottom conditions. The casualness is the craft.

jokes.text: You know what would be really nuts? I was thinking maybe we could split an app or, like, get a charcuterie plate. But I don't think we need to do anything too... crazy.
jokes.setup: Ilana is either talking to a coworker or setting up the competitive night — the context of 'we could split an app' being 'really nuts' is the joke.
jokes.punchline: Splitting an appetizer as her definition of 'really nuts' when she is a broke person working at a restaurant — the gap between her framing ('really nuts,' 'crazy') and the actual tameness of the suggestion — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A solid characterization joke about Ilana's financial reality clashing with her self-image as someone who lives large.

jokes.text: The dress. [visual beat of Ilana in a striking outfit]
jokes.setup: Cut to Ilana revealing her work outfit for the competitive tip night.
jokes.punchline: The dress is presumably so over-the-top that it functions as a visual gag — Ilana treating a waitress shift like a red carpet event. Her 'Bada boom, Marcel' line confirms her confidence in it.
jokes.explanation: In a visual medium, Ilana's outfit choices are a recurring comedy device. The 'the dress' moment — cut to her reveal — is designed as a visual punchline. Rewatch bonus because the full impact depends on what the dress looks like.

jokes.text: Smells like a winning kind of night, baby! I'm glad you think so, tiny tits.
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives at the restaurant, confident in her dress.
jokes.punchline: Marcel's response — 'tiny tits' — is a casually deployed insult-as-nickname that both deflates Ilana's confidence and immediately establishes Marcel's abrasive management style.
jokes.explanation: Marcel as a chaos manager is a reliable engine. The deadpan cruelty of 'tiny tits' as a greeting undercuts Ilana's entrance perfectly.

jokes.text: You are an AMILF, Abbi's mom, I'd like to... Friend! Oh. Friend! Yeah, friends. We're just friends. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Ilana sees Abbi's mom in her blue dress and begins the acronym for 'MILF,' but catches herself.
jokes.punchline: She replaces the expected final word with 'Friend' — which is obviously not what 'AMILF' would stand for, making the substitution funnier than the original. Both characters then over-confirm 'friends' repeatedly.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the pivot — 'Friend' as the replacement F-word is both transparently false and the only possible socially acceptable landing. The repetitive confirmation spiral ('Yeah, friends. We're just friends') escalates the awkwardness nicely. High impact because audiences always respond to the barely-averted MILF joke.

jokes.text: There's my favorite ass. / Joanne? Oh. / Look at you, Joanne. / Hey, pretty girl. / Wow, Mama, you look so hot. / Your body is amazing, dude! / Really?
jokes.setup: Ilana greets Joanne at the restaurant with the enthusiasm she'd reserve for a celebrity.
jokes.punchline: Ilana calling Abbi's mom 'Mama' and telling her 'your body is amazing, dude' — the breakdown of normal mother/friend of daughter social barriers is funny and slightly cringe-worthy. Joanne's 'Really?' — pure delight — makes it warmer.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's complete lack of generational or social decorum with Abbi's mom is very on-brand. The 'your body is amazing, dude' line lands because it's the kind of thing you say to a peer, not your friend's middle-aged mother. Character integration high — only Ilana does this.

jokes.text: Well, let me take you to your table before I, uh, do something really weird.
jokes.setup: Ilana has just barely stopped herself from calling Abbi's mom an AMILF.
jokes.punchline: She self-aware acknowledgment that she is about to 'do something really weird' — delivered as a professional waiter line — is the admission of impulse control failure dressed as hospitality.
jokes.explanation: Strong button on the AMILF sequence. The self-awareness makes it funnier than if Ilana had just been oblivious.

jokes.text: What is good to drink here? Oh, um, there's, uh, an espresso matcha tea, if you wanna get a little buzzy. What is that? Oh, this is, uh, the Mambeaux-Tini. It's our signature cocktail. It has alcohol in it.
jokes.setup: Joanne asks what's good to drink; Ilana as waiter explains the cocktail.
jokes.punchline: 'It has alcohol in it' as the defining description of a restaurant's signature cocktail — as if this is a notable feature worth mentioning — is a perfectly deflating waiter recommendation.
jokes.explanation: 'It has alcohol in it' is a great low-key joke. As a selling point for a cocktail it's technically accurate but wholly inadequate — the joke is in Ilana's implicit acknowledgment that she doesn't know what's in it.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! Bad girls! Yeow! Let's do shots. I have never done that. Um... Okay, yeah, let's do shots, I guess.
jokes.setup: Joanne reacts to being called a 'bad girl' and immediately proposes shots.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's 'Okay, yeah, let's do shots, I guess' — the resigned acceptance of a child enabling a parent's newfound wildness — is the joke. The role reversal is complete.
jokes.explanation: The parent-child reversal is the engine of much of this episode's comedy and it's running here. Abbi's flat 'I guess' is the key word — the sigh of someone who has lost control of the situation.

jokes.text: Well, you know, I don't think I've had hard liquor s... Well, since I got pregnant with you.
jokes.setup: Joanne is about to do a shot with Abbi. She begins the sentence as a statement of abstinence.
jokes.punchline: The interruption of her own sentence and the redirect to 'since I got pregnant with you' implies she drank heavily right up to the moment of conception — a small dark revelation dressed as reassurance.
jokes.explanation: The mid-sentence self-correction is the key technique here. She catches herself about to say something worse, pivots, but the pivot itself is damning. The implication — she was drinking right up to the pregnancy — is revealed without being stated. Good economy and structure.

jokes.text: Did you finish it? Why do people do this? I don't think anyone knows. It's a mystery.
jokes.setup: They've both done shots. Joanne clearly did not enjoy it.
jokes.punchline: Joanne's philosophical bewilderment ('Why do people do this?') and Abbi's shrug response ('I don't think anyone knows. It's a mystery') is both a shared moment and a good observational joke — she's right, the cultural logic of shots is inexplicable.
jokes.explanation: A small gem. The combination of Joanne's genuine confusion and Abbi's honest acknowledgment that she doesn't have a better answer is funnier than if Abbi had tried to justify it. 'It's a mystery' is the right landing.

jokes.text: Let's do another one.
jokes.setup: Joanne has just reacted to the shot with total confusion about why anyone does this.
jokes.punchline: Her immediate pivot to 'Let's do another one' — despite hating it — is a perfect encapsulation of her bucket list energy and the human stubbornness of bad decisions.
jokes.explanation: Four words that pay off the previous joke perfectly. The callback to 'it's a mystery' makes Joanne's insistence on another shot funnier — she has resolved nothing and is doubling down.

jokes.text: Well, uh, you all look like you have money to burn and machismo to measure.
jokes.setup: A waiter approaches a table of men to sell them caviar.
jokes.punchline: 'Machismo to measure' is a bizarre, specific phrase for a waiter to use — it's slightly threatening, slightly flattering, and entirely weird. It's upselling through coded masculinity challenge.
jokes.explanation: The phrase is funny because it's so specifically wrong as a sales technique. 'Machismo to measure' is the kind of absurdist waiter patter that feels like a Broad City background joke. Quirky but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Let's just say they eat Sushi Mambeaux caviar in Michelin!
jokes.setup: The waiter is pitching the restaurant's award-winning caviar.
jokes.punchline: The Michelin Guide is famous for awarding stars to restaurants, not eating food — saying 'they eat Sushi Mambeaux caviar in Michelin' inverts the relationship entirely, as if the Michelin organization is a diner rather than a critic.
jokes.explanation: A good absurdist restaurant joke. The specific wrongness of the Michelin claim — they don't 'eat' there, they rate there — suggests this waiter has memorized a pitch he doesn't fully understand.

jokes.text: All winter... And fall. The end of summer too.
jokes.setup: Ilana is apparently overhearing/narrating something, or the scene cuts to her in a confessional moment.
jokes.punchline: This reprises her earlier expanding list of depression seasons — the callback confirms the joke was earned and the running gag of Ilana's depression covering more of the year than not is intact.
jokes.explanation: The callback works because it's verbatim — she's repeating herself, which suggests this is a canned speech she gives often. Callbacks that feel like character revelation rather than just repetition earn their score.

jokes.text: You know, move. It's, like, uh, pigs eating slop out of a trough at a country fair. Being measured for our weight in bacon.
jokes.setup: Ilana is explaining what family-style dining looks like — or describing her coworkers/competitors eating.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'pigs eating slop' to 'being measured for our weight in bacon' is a surreal, dark image — it's an increasingly specific and grim metaphor for restaurant work culture.
jokes.explanation: 'Being measured for our weight in bacon' is the kind of absurdist escalation Broad City does well — it's specific, dark, and weird enough to be funny without being cruel.

jokes.text: Please work, please work, please work... [beat] Ilana? Jesus. Dude, is this as bright as it was before? I'm not feeling it! Uh, it's pretty bright. It's just not doing it for me, man. I can't get the juice.
jokes.setup: Ilana retreats to a back area to use her SAD lamp during the shift.
jokes.punchline: The SAD lamp — established as Ilana's magic drug in the opening — is now failing to deliver. Her desperate 'please work, please work' and 'I can't get the juice' frames the therapy lamp as an addiction that's losing its potency.
jokes.explanation: Strong running gag payoff. 'I can't get the juice' is a great line — 'the juice' as metaphor for the dopamine hit from the lamp is funny and specific. The escalation of Ilana's SAD lamp dependency deepens the episode's mental health subplot.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. Oh, keep 'er coming. Oh, my God, that is amazing. Jeez! Oh, that's good. Ass of an angel. Mind of mental health MacGyver!
jokes.setup: Abbi apparently helps Ilana rig the SAD lamp to be brighter or in a better position.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's ecstatic response — orgasmic moaning at a therapy lamp, culminating in 'Ass of an angel. Mind of mental health MacGyver!' — is both a physical comedy bit and a great compliment for Abbi.
jokes.explanation: 'Mental health MacGyver' is the line of the episode so far. It's original, it's specific to Broad City's world, it's a genuine compliment dressed as absurdity, and it works as character praise. 'Ass of an angel' as a double compliment also lands perfectly. High quotability because both phrases are genuinely memorable.

jokes.text: Okay, no, listen, my mom, like, apparently had a lump in her breast, and she didn't tell me about it. It's fine now, it's fine. But, like, it really scared the shit out of me.
jokes.setup: Abbi interrupts the lamp celebration to process her feelings about her mom's health scare.
jokes.punchline: The tonal shift from Ilana's lamp ecstasy to Abbi's emotional vulnerability is the joke — the whiplash is the comedy, and Ilana's responsiveness to Abbi even in the middle of her own crisis is the character beat.
jokes.explanation: This is more emotional than comedic, but the context creates gentle humor through contrast. Not a strong joke in isolation but necessary emotional infrastructure.

jokes.text: I know, I have a timeless classic for you. Y'amagine?
jokes.setup: Ilana is helping Abbi plan how to give her mom a great night.
jokes.punchline: 'Y'amagine?' — Ilana's compressed 'you can imagine' or 'can you imagine' into a single slurred syllable — is a verbal tic that functions as character comedy. She delivers it with complete confidence as if it's a coherent word.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's verbal innovations are a character hallmark. 'Y'amagine' is a small but effective one.

jokes.text: You really know your way around that stuff, huh? Not really. Okay, I smoke, like, sometimes. All right, I smoke... I smoke a lot.
jokes.setup: Ilana is rolling a joint for Abbi and her mom, and Abbi compliments her skill.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's gradual, pressured honesty — 'sometimes... I smoke a lot' — mirrors the earlier medication confession. She doesn't lie as much as she slowly reveals truth under mild questioning.
jokes.explanation: The escalation mirrors the medication joke structurally — Ilana starts with minimization and arrives at honesty. The brevity of each step makes it work.

jokes.text: I'm gonna light it, and you're gonna take a gasp like Dad asked you to get back together.
jokes.setup: Ilana is coaching Joanne on how to smoke weed for the first time.
jokes.punchline: She uses the emotional reference point of Joanne's divorced/estranged husband asking to reconcile — a deeply specific, slightly cruel emotional trigger — as the benchmark for how deeply to inhale. It reveals both Ilana's intimacy with Abbi's family history and her complete lack of filter.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best line and one of the better Broad City jokes in the series. It's operating on multiple levels simultaneously: (1) it's a functional weed-smoking instruction, (2) it reveals Ilana knows Joanne's emotional history with her ex-husband, (3) it's breathtakingly blunt about using a woman's suppressed romantic longing as a respiratory metaphor, (4) and it works because the image of a desperate gasp of longing is actually a precise physical instruction for how to hit a joint. The character integration is exceptional — only Ilana would think of this framing, and only in this episode does it have context. Genuinely close to the 8.0 anchor and above it.

jokes.text: Not I think about that at night.
jokes.setup: Ilana has used the 'Dad asking to get back together' reference as a smoking instruction.
jokes.punchline: She immediately clarifies she doesn't think about it — but the clarification itself ('Not I think about that at night') is so oddly phrased and insistent that it implies she does.
jokes.explanation: The overcorrection joke. She protests too much. The grammatically odd phrasing ('Not I think about that at night') also suggests improv-born authenticity — it sounds like a real verbal stumble that reveals more than intended.

jokes.text: My head feels like a balloon. Like one of those allergy commercials.
jokes.setup: Joanne has taken her first hit of marijuana.
jokes.punchline: Her reference point for her altered state is an allergy commercial — a deeply suburban, TV-informed way of understanding drug intoxication. It's the perfect detail to characterize a conservative woman experiencing weed for the first time.
jokes.explanation: This is a great character joke. The specific reference — not 'my head feels weird' but 'like one of those allergy commercials' — tells us everything about Joanne's cultural framework. She is experiencing something outside her vocabulary and reaches for the nearest TV image. The specificity earns it.

jokes.text: You know who else I think smokes a lot of this? Who? Charlie Rose. Oh, my God. That voice and the way he shoots the show like he's in a black hole. Yeah, there's no walls. Right. It's just table. God, I would not wanna (bleep) him.
jokes.setup: Joanne is high for the first time and making connections.
jokes.punchline: Her stoned theory that Charlie Rose smokes weed — supported by 'there's no walls, it's just table' and her PBS show aesthetic analysis — followed by 'God, I would not wanna fuck him' as a non-sequitur conclusion is a perfect high-person monologue. The 'just table' observation is actually kind of insightful.
jokes.explanation: This sequence is genuinely excellent. 'It's just table' as a TV production observation is both funny as an artifact of high-brain analysis and weirdly accurate as a description of the Charlie Rose set. The pivot to 'God, I would not want to fuck him' as the logical conclusion to a weed conspiracy theory is perfect — it's the precise non-sequitur that a newly-high conservative mom would land on. High quotability and character-specific to Joanne's newly unleashed candor.

jokes.text: Dude, I don't think I've ever heard you say (bleep) like that before. Well, there it is.
jokes.setup: Joanne just said she wouldn't want to fuck Charlie Rose — an unusually crude statement for her.
jokes.punchline: Joanne's 'Well, there it is' — delivered as a simple acknowledgment of her own transformation — is perfectly calm. It's as if she has been waiting her whole life to say 'fuck' and is registering it as a milestone.
jokes.explanation: 'Well, there it is' is a near-perfect button. Three words that carry enormous weight — it's a first-time profanity milestone treated with quiet dignity. The economy is exceptional and it's deeply character-specific to Joanne's transformation arc in this episode.

jokes.text: It's so funny for you to call me a dude. Well, not, like, a dude. Just, like, dude. Like, anyone can be a dude. It's just like a friend thing. Aww... Well, then, cool... dude.
jokes.setup: Abbi has been calling Joanne 'dude' — her natural speech pattern — and Joanne finds it amusing.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's anxious over-explanation of what 'dude' means ('not, like, a dude... anyone can be a dude') and Joanne's warm adoption of it ('cool... dude') is a small generational/linguistic bonding moment played for gentle comedy.
jokes.explanation: Sweet rather than hilarious. Joanne saying 'cool... dude' for the first time lands warmly. The character integration for both is strong.

jokes.text: Let me tell you something. Life is trash. [extended pause/beat]
jokes.setup: Ilana is at work, apparently mid-shift, addressing someone (or the camera).
jokes.punchline: The flat declaration 'Life is trash' followed by a beat of silence is Ilana's seasonal depression manifesting as profound aphorism. The context — she's a waitress at a fancy sushi restaurant — makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: Four words that function as a thesis statement for Ilana's winter depression storyline. The economy is perfect and the beat after it amplifies the joke. It's been earned by everything that preceded it.

jokes.text: It just tastes better. Like, stuff just tastes better, right? / I'm pot.
jokes.setup: Joanne, extremely high, makes observations about food.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm pot.' — Joanne has become so high she has grammatically confused herself with the substance, or possibly is declaring her identity as the drug itself. Either interpretation is funny.
jokes.explanation: Two words. 'I'm pot.' is the funniest line in the episode. It's absurd, unparseable, deeply committed, and perfectly delivered as a non-sequitur after a lucid observation about food tasting better. A first-time smoker so high she can no longer distinguish herself from the substance is both philosophically interesting and hilarious. Economy is near-perfect. The quotability is off the charts — it's a completely ownable phrase. This is an episode highlight.

jokes.text: How many men have you slept with? Whoa, okay, um... I don't know if I wanna, like...
jokes.setup: Abbi and her mom are high together; Joanne asks the one question adult children dread from parents.
jokes.punchline: The question itself is the setup for the escalating confession sequence that follows — Joanne's pot-emboldened honesty being directed at her daughter is the cringe engine.
jokes.explanation: The setup for the episode's most sustained comedic sequence. The joke is Abbi's visible panic at the question.

jokes.text: Paul Kafferty, my first boyfriend who I think was gay anyway. Your father. And now Neil. Okay. My 'husband,' Neil. You are married.
jokes.setup: Joanne is listing her three sexual partners.
jokes.punchline: The list is funny for its specificity: (1) Paul Kafferty, named, suspected gay; (2) Abbi's unnamed father; (3) Neil, her current husband, referred to with air-quotes energy by Abbi ('You are married'). The casual hedging of the first partner ('I think was gay anyway') is both sympathetic and bleak.
jokes.explanation: Paul Kafferty is a great throwaway character name, and 'I think was gay anyway' is a characteristically sad, deflective rationalization. The structure of the list — where each partner is worse than the last — is an escalation joke dressed as exposition.

jokes.text: We haven't made love in over a month. And if we're being honest, it's more like making like.
jokes.setup: Joanne is describing her marriage to Neil.
jokes.punchline: 'Making like' as a degradation of 'making love' — where they go through the motions without genuine feeling — is a clever, sad turn of phrase that functions as both wordplay and a bleak relationship revelation.
jokes.explanation: 'Making like' is genuinely excellent writing. It's economical wordplay that carries enormous emotional weight — a woman describing the hollowness of her intimate life with her husband in two words. It's funny and devastating simultaneously. The craft is high: original coinage, perfect economy, character-specific, and the 'earned' quality is high because it comes from genuine character revelation, not a cheap shock. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: I just wish I would've (bleep) up a little more. I wish I would've (bleep) a little more.
jokes.setup: Joanne has been describing her sexually constrained life.
jokes.punchline: She first says she wishes she'd 'fucked up a little more,' then immediately self-corrects to 'fucked a little more' — the correction revealing the original phrase was actually the more interesting one (both versions apply).
jokes.explanation: The self-correction joke is structurally clever — 'fucked up a little more' is actually the truer statement, and correcting it to 'fucked a little more' makes both versions simultaneously true. The structure builds in a second beat of laughter after the first. High character integration because this could only come from Joanne at this specific moment of vulnerable honesty.

jokes.text: I never had sex with a man who didn't speak English. Not even an ESL guy.
jokes.setup: Joanne is listing her sexual regrets.
jokes.punchline: 'Not even an ESL guy' — as a clarifying downgrade of her aspiration — is the joke. She's not asking for much, just someone with basic functional English, and she hasn't even achieved that.
jokes.explanation: 'Not even an ESL guy' is the kind of specific lowering of the bar that makes a joke. The two-step — 'non-English speaker' then downgraded to 'ESL' — follows the same structure as Joanne's list in joke 20. It's character-consistent and quietly hilarious.

jokes.text: You know, I've never even had anal sex. I mean, not even a finger. I mean, it's not really something you can ask for. Well, you just have to actually just ask for it.
jokes.setup: Joanne continues her list of sexual regrets with her daughter.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's helpful, matter-of-fact advice to 'just ask for it' — delivered without a beat — is the punchline. She is coaching her mother on how to request anal sex with the same energy she might explain how to get a good table at a restaurant.
jokes.explanation: The joke is Abbi's pragmatic matter-of-factness. She doesn't flinch, she doesn't lecture, she doesn't express horror — she just gives useful advice. The cringe is that this is still her mother. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Last week, I penetrated myself with a bottle of cough syrup.
jokes.setup: The mother-daughter honesty session has been escalating; Abbi has just said it's 'okay' about the anal thing.
jokes.punchline: Joanne's TMI revelation — specific, clinical, and completely delivered without apparent shame — is the spike of the escalation. The word 'penetrated' doing clinical work, and 'cough syrup' as the implement, makes it maximally specific and therefore maximally funny.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's highest-impact moment and one of Broad City's best individual jokes. It earns its score: (1) structurally, it is the perfect escalation endpoint — each revelation has prepared the audience for this spike; (2) the specificity is exquisite — not a generic object but cough syrup, which implies it was whatever was in the medicine cabinet, implying desperation and improvisation; (3) 'penetrated' as a clinical verb is funnier than any colloquial alternative; (4) it's deeply character-specific — Joanne's matter-of-fact delivery makes it land harder than if she were embarrassed; (5) Abbi's subsequent reaction ('Okay. Get out of the way!') is perfect. Comparable to and arguably approaching the 9.5 anchor example in terms of character-specific shock escalation.

jokes.text: Okay. Get out of the way! [Abbi exits rapidly]
jokes.setup: Her mother has just told her about the cough syrup.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's immediate, wordless flight — the total shutdown of the conversation through physical evacuation — is the perfect button. She doesn't process it, she just leaves.
jokes.explanation: The reaction beat earns an 8 because the structure is almost perfect. Abbi saying 'Okay' (accepting it), then 'Get out of the way' (redirecting to action) is the ideal response to information too large to process. The physical exit is funnier than any verbal comeback. Reaction beats depend entirely on structure, and this one is textbook.

jokes.text: Dude, your tinfoil is broke! Okay, uh, dude, my mom just told me she's, like, really sexually frustrated and that she (bleep) a bottle of cough syrup last week.
jokes.setup: Abbi finds Ilana in the back struggling with her SAD lamp.
jokes.punchline: Abbi relaying the cough syrup revelation to Ilana — who is simultaneously dealing with her own SAD lamp crisis — creates a chaos collision of two disasters. Ilana's response will be the next joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the juxtaposition of two crises meeting. Abbi's flat recitation of the cough syrup story — while Ilana is panicking about lightbulbs — is good structure.

jokes.text: I'm, like, I don't know if I can, like, handle this level of honesty. I'm, like, is this what being an adult is 'cause this is terrifying.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just relayed the cough syrup story to Ilana.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's genuine existential crisis — 'is this what being an adult is?' — functions as the show's thesis statement for the episode. Her framing of adult honesty as 'terrifying' is both the joke and the truth.
jokes.explanation: This is a good character moment that functions comedically. 'Is this what being an adult is' is the kind of line that resonates with the show's audience precisely because it's true.

jokes.text: That is really, really... Really, really sad. That is so sad. Okay, dude, dude, dude, what do you need from me? Bulbs.
jokes.setup: Ilana is processing the cough syrup information.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's genuine empathy for Joanne ('that is so sad') followed immediately by snapping into problem-solving mode ('what do you need from me? Bulbs.') — the pivot from emotional processing to logistical crisis is perfect Ilana.
jokes.explanation: The whiplash from 'that is so sad' to 'Bulbs' (as in lightbulbs for the SAD lamp) is perfect economy. Ilana is simultaneously the most emotionally available and the most self-absorbed character, and this captures both.

jokes.text: Ahh! Oh! Okay, here we go. O-M-G! Serotonin rising. Dopamine flowing! Yes! Oh! It's working, dude, it's working.
jokes.setup: Abbi has found new bulbs and repaired the SAD lamp.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's ecstatic response — now explicitly naming neurotransmitters ('Serotonin rising. Dopamine flowing!') — reprises joke 7 with escalated pseudo-scientific framing, as if she is monitoring her own brain chemistry.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the lamp reaction from pure visceral ecstasy to named neurotransmitters is a good running gag development. 'Serotonin rising. Dopamine flowing!' is quotable.

jokes.text: Whoo! 32! My daughter fucked 32 guys. Oh, my God. Oh, God, no, please, no.
jokes.setup: Ilana, in her lamp-induced euphoria, announces to the restaurant.
jokes.punchline: Ilana, lit up by the SAD lamp, shouts Abbi's number to the restaurant. The callback to the '32' reveal is now weaponized as public humiliation. Abbi's 'Oh, God, no, please, no' is the button.
jokes.explanation: A well-executed callback with genuine comedic escalation. The '32' number was a private revelation; Ilana making it public in a restaurant by announcing it mid-ecstasy is a perfect Ilana action. The structural payoff of the number joke landing in the most embarrassing possible context is satisfying.

jokes.text: I'm pot!
jokes.setup: Joanne is high for the first time and is apparently in the middle of the restaurant chaos.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm pot' — her entire self-identification reduced to the substance she has taken — is a bizarre, simple statement that functions as a perfect high-person non-sequitur. She is not saying she smokes pot; she is declaring she has become pot.
jokes.explanation: Two words, maximum impact. 'I'm pot' is one of the best pure lines in the episode. The absurdist logic — you are what you consume — delivered as simple declarative statement by a freshly-high 55-year-old conservative mom is almost perfectly formed. High quotability, high character integration. This earns its 8+ score: it's economically flawless, character-specific, and delivers a laugh disproportionate to its length.

jokes.text: Look, I don't know what kind of Strong Island honkies you brought up in here, Other Tonya, but this is not Billy Joel's go-to Dave and Buster's, okay?
jokes.setup: Marcel is dealing with the chaos Abbi's mom has helped create in the restaurant.
jokes.punchline: 'Billy Joel's go-to Dave and Buster's' as a description of what this restaurant is NOT is the joke — it's a brilliant specificity of suburban Long Island culture (Strong Island) mashed against an upscale sushi restaurant. The phrase 'Other Tonya' as an address for someone whose name he doesn't know is also a small gem.
jokes.explanation: This line earns its 8+ score through sheer specificity. 'Strong Island honkies' is a precise cultural category (Long Island white suburban types). 'Billy Joel's go-to Dave and Buster's' is original and specific comedy — it's a phrase that creates an entire fictional suburban landscape in six words. 'Other Tonya' is a perfect throwaway character naming convention. Marcel as a character is at his best when his insults are this precisely aimed.

jokes.text: I'm, I'm sorry, Marcel. I wasn't on top of it, and I just... don't care. I'm feeling really depressed from the winter, and I'm just... numb. Do what you gotta do. I honestly don't give a shit. You should fire me.
jokes.setup: Marcel is confronting Ilana about her failure to manage the table.
jokes.punchline: Rather than apologizing or making excuses, Ilana fully embraces her depression as the reason for professional failure and invites her own firing. The complete surrender — 'You should fire me' — is both funny and genuinely sympathetic.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's depression manifesting as professional nihilism is consistent with the episode's throughline. 'You should fire me' delivered without self-pity, as a genuine suggestion, is a strong character moment and a funny one.

jokes.text: Oh, okay, you play... You playin' me right now, ain't you? Marcel, seriously. Take my tips. Do whatever you gotta do. Oh, please, man, you are a... You are a career hustler. Whatever it is you're doing is working on me.
jokes.setup: Marcel suspects Ilana is performing helplessness to avoid being fired.
jokes.punchline: Marcel accuses Ilana of being a 'career hustler' who is playing him — while Ilana is genuinely depressed and not hustling at all. The irony is Marcel's savvy cynicism being completely wrong in this case.
jokes.explanation: Marcel's read on Ilana is entirely wrong — which is funnier than if he were right. The experienced manager being completely fooled by authentic depression is the joke.

jokes.text: You know what? I want you to pick (bleep) me. Get in there. Go on! Wait a minute? Are you for real?
jokes.setup: Marcel is deciding what to do with Ilana after concluding she is a hustler who deserves a reward.
jokes.punchline: Marcel's decision to have Ilana 'pick' him — a vague but clearly inappropriate workplace proposition — and Ilana's genuine surprise ('Are you for real?') inverts power dynamics in a Marcel-specific way.
jokes.explanation: Marcel's character is built on transgressive management behavior but this beat is less specifically funny than his better lines. Works contextually but not exceptional.

jokes.text: But this depression shit. That's, that's next level bitchy. I'm kind of digging it. My dick is a little bit hard. I hope you never get better.
jokes.setup: Marcel is explaining why he appreciates Ilana's depressive state.
jokes.punchline: 'I hope you never get better' as a form of managerial encouragement — delivered after saying depression is 'next level bitchy' and admitting physical arousal — is Marcel's most character-specific speech in the episode.
jokes.explanation: 'I hope you never get better' is a brilliant line in context. As workplace encouragement it is maximally wrong; as a perverse compliment it makes complete sense within Marcel's character. The escalation through 'bitchy,' arousal, and 'never get better' builds to the punchline efficiently.

jokes.text: Thanks.
jokes.setup: Marcel has just said 'I hope you never get better' about her depression.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's single-word 'Thanks' — accepting this as a genuine compliment — is the perfect button. She is so genuinely numb that inappropriate encouragement reads as normal.
jokes.explanation: One word. The entire joke is the single word 'Thanks' in response to 'I hope you never get better.' The character integration is nearly perfect — Ilana's depression making her genuinely appreciate this backwards compliment is both funny and heartbreaking. Economy score is 10 because it cannot be reduced further. This earns its 8+ score for structural perfection and complete character integration.

jokes.text: Mom! Mom! Oh, my God, Mom, stop it! What, Abbi, go! Are you kidding me right now! Stop it! Oh! This slut! Again? Ha! He called me a slut! Yes!
jokes.setup: Joanne is apparently in a physical altercation of some kind — possibly dancing or fighting — in or near the restaurant.
jokes.punchline: Joanne being called a 'slut' by a stranger and reacting with pure delight ('Yes!') — treating it as an achievement rather than an insult — is the punchline. Her bucket list energy has reached street-level chaos.
jokes.explanation: Joanne's 'Yes!' as a response to being called a slut is the episode's best character payoff so far. It's the culmination of her transformation arc — she has gone from conservative suburban mom to someone who considers being called a slut a personal victory. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I need to go back to med school. And rehab. Owen! Owen! Owen, you're fired!
jokes.setup: Marcel is witnessing the chaos in and around the restaurant.
jokes.punchline: 'I need to go back to med school. And rehab.' — said in that order, suggesting his journey from rehab would be a prerequisite for med school — followed immediately by firing Owen in the middle of his personal crisis is a perfect Marcel multi-task.
jokes.explanation: The callback to firing Owen is satisfying — we've been waiting for it. 'I need to go back to med school and rehab' implies Marcel has an entire backstory of failed professional attempts, delivered in the middle of a crisis. The multi-tasking of personal revelation and firing is very Marcel.

jokes.text: I need to go back to med school. / And rehab. / Owen! Owen! Owen, you're fired!
jokes.setup: The chaos outside the restaurant reaches a peak. Marcel appears.
jokes.punchline: Someone declares they need to go back to med school AND rehab — two institutions that imply very different life trajectories. Marcel, ignoring all context, fires Owen.
jokes.explanation: Med school AND rehab is funny because they're completely incompatible life trajectories stated together. Marcel firing Owen mid-chaos as if this is the one thing requiring administrative action is good absurdist comedy.

jokes.text: Marcel! I blew you, dude! And? (Bleep) this! I'm out.
jokes.setup: Owen has just been fired and pleads his case.
jokes.punchline: Owen's protest is that he performed a sexual act for Marcel ('I blew you, dude!') — and Marcel's response is a single, flat 'And?' — implying this was insufficient grounds for job retention and creates a disturbing picture of the restaurant's employment practices.
jokes.explanation: Marcel's 'And?' is devastating. The implication — that a sexual favor doesn't get you employment security — is both dark and funny. Economy is excellent. 'And?' as a complete response to 'I blew you' does enormous work in one syllable.

jokes.text: I kissed a bisexual man with tongue.
jokes.setup: The chaos of the night is resolving; Joanne reports on her accomplishments.
jokes.punchline: Joanne's proud announcement that she kissed a bisexual man with tongue — as if ticking a box on a bucket list — is the culmination of her transformation arc, and the specificity of 'bisexual man' (not just 'a man') is the joke detail.
jokes.explanation: This is a satisfying bucket list payoff. 'A bisexual man with tongue' is a highly specific achievement, and the pride with which Joanne announces it is the joke. 'With tongue' as a qualifier — as if a kiss without tongue would count for less — is excellent specificity. High quotability.

jokes.text: Sushi Mambeaux. / Always celebrating cultural, fluid exchanges.
jokes.setup: In the aftermath of complete chaos, someone (Ilana in waiter character or an implied restaurant tagline) drops the Sushi Mambeaux motto.
jokes.punchline: 'Always celebrating cultural, fluid exchanges' — the restaurant's pretentious tagline accidentally perfectly describes the night's actual events (bisexual kissing, cross-cultural desire, general chaos).
jokes.explanation: 'Fluid exchanges' doing double duty — literal cultural exchange and sexual fluidity — is a nice piece of wordplay. The irony of the restaurant's corporate language inadvertently being accurate is a satisfying button on the chaos.

jokes.text: Your ass is fired too! [shouted into distance]
jokes.setup: Marcel has just fired his apparent child.
jokes.punchline: He shouts it again into the distance — ensuring it registered. The redundancy is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A button that works because of Marcel's commitment. Shouting the firing again ensures we know he means it. Gets diminishing returns from the original joke but still funny as a character beat.
